items.q: Are the client and the user always the same person?
items.correct: No — the client commissions and often pays for the design, while users are the people who actually interact with the product; their needs can differ or even conflict.
items.misconception: Whoever pays for the design is automatically the user.
items.random_wrong: The user is the person who manufactures the product.
items.why: Students collapse all stakeholders into one figure; separating client, user and wider stakeholders is what makes requirement analysis meaningful.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary research?
items.correct: Primary research is first-hand data the designer collects, such as interviews and observation; secondary research uses existing published sources like reports and websites.
items.misconception: Primary research means the most important research, whatever its source.
items.random_wrong: Secondary research is any research done by a second member of the team.
items.why: Students interpret primary/secondary as a ranking of importance rather than a distinction between first-hand and existing data.

items.q: Is 'the product must look nice' an acceptable specification point?
items.correct: No — specification criteria should be measurable and testable, such as 'must weigh under 500 g' or 'must cost under 15 pounds to manufacture'.
items.misconception: Vague aspirations are fine in a specification as long as they sound positive.
items.random_wrong: A specification may only list the materials to be used.
items.why: Students write wish-lists instead of testable criteria; measurable points are what make evaluation against the specification possible later.

items.q: Which of these is primary research?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Interviewing users about a product. “Reading a market report online” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who counts as a stakeholder in a design project?
items.correct: Anyone affected by the product, such as users, clients and retailers
items.misconception: Only the person who pays for the design
items.why: Correct answer: Anyone affected by the product, such as users, clients and retailers. “Only the person who pays for the design” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Placing the end user's needs at the heart of every design decision
items.misconception: Letting users manufacture the product themselves
items.random_wrong: Designing only products users already own
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Placing the end user's needs at the heart of every design decision. “Letting users manufacture the product themselves” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why explore the design context before writing a brief?
items.correct: To understand the real needs and constraints of the situation
items.misconception: To choose the product's final colour
items.why: Correct answer: To understand the real needs and constraints of the situation. “To choose the product's final colour” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
